title: 第 37 節
en: Avidy&, Nescience (5).

XIV. What is the fivefold Avidya (Nescience)? It is Tamas, darkless, Moha, illusion, Mahamoha, great illusion, Tamisra, gloom, Andhatamisra, utter glooin. Here darkness and illusion are again each eightfold, great illusion is tenfold, gloom and utter gloom are eighteenfold. Tamas, darkness, is the misconception that Self is identical with things which are not Self, namely with Prakriti, Avyakta, Buddhi, Ahamkara, and the five Tanmatras. Moha, illusion, is the misconception arising from the obtainment of supernatural powers, such as minuteness and the rest. Mahamoha, great illusion, is when one supposes oneself to be liberated in the ten states with regard to the objects of sound, colour, &c., whether heard or seen, £c. Gloom is unrestrained hatred, directed against the eightfold superhuman powers, such as minuteness, &c., and against the tenfold world of sense causing threefold pain. Utter gloom is that distress which arises at the time of death after the eightfold human power has been acquired, and the tenfold world of sense has been conquered. Thus has ignorance with sixty-two subdivisions been explained.
Asakti, Weakness (28).

XV. What is called the twenty-eightfold weakness? The faults of the eleven organs of sense and the seventeen faults of the intellect. First, with regard to the organs of sense, there is deafness in the ear, dullness in the tongue, leprosy in the skin, blindness in the eye, loss of smell in the nose, dumbness in the voice, crippledness in the hands, lameness in the feet, constipation in the organ of excretion, impotence in the organ of generation, madness in the mind ; these are defects of the eleven organs. The seventeen defects of the intellect are the opposites of the Tushtfis, contentments, and of the Siddhis, perfections.

ATUSE27! AND TUSETI. ASIDDHIS AND SIDDEIS. 269 Atusliii and XVI. First then the opposites of the Tushiis or the contentments. They are Ananta, the conviction that there is no Pradhana (Prakriti) ; Tamasalina, consisting in recognising the Atman in the Mahat (Buddhi, intellect); Avidytl, the non-recognition of the Ego (Ahamkara) ; Avrishfi, the denial that the Tanmatras, essences, are the causes of the elements ; Asutara, occupation in acquiring the objects of the senses ; Asupara, occupation in their preservation ; Asunetra, occupation for wealth, without seeing that it is liable to be lost ; Asumari&ika, addiction to enjoyment ; Anuttamambhasika, engaging in enjoyment without seeing the evil of injury (to living beings). Thus have the nine opposites of Tushii, contentment, been explained.
AsiddMs and Siddhis.

XVII. Next follow the opposites of Siddhi, perfection, which are also called Asiddhis, noivperf ections : Atara, when diversity is mistaken for phenomenal unity ; Sutara, when, after hearing words only, the opposite is understood, as, for instance, when after hearing that a man who knows the various principles (tattvas) is liberated, a man understands the opposite, that such a man is not liberated ; Ataratara, ignorance, when a man, though devoted to hearing and studying, does not succeed in knowing the twenty-five principles, owing either to his obtuseness or to his intellect being impaired by false doctrines. If a man, though overcome by mental suffering, is not anxious to know, being careless as to transmigration, so that knowledge is no pleasure to him, this is Apramoda. Thus the next pair also of Apramudita (mutually not delighted) and Apramodamana (mutually not delighting) should be considered. Ignorance of a man of undecided mind even with regard to what has been taught him by a friend is Arasya. Put failure of an unfortunate man in obtaining knowledge, either because of bad instruction or disregard on the part of the teacher, is Asatpramuditam. Thus have the eight Asiddhis, the opposite of the Siddhis or perfections, been explained, and the twenty-eightfold Asakti (weakness) is finished.
Tush/is and SiddMs.

Next follow the Tushiis and Siddhis themselves, but as their opposltes have already been examined we may dispense with their enumeration here. Some of these technical terms vary in different texts, but they are of very small importance \ I am afraid that even what I have given of these long lists, which are so characteristic of the S&mkhyaphilosophy, may have proved very tedious, and not very closely connected with the great problems of philosophy. I confess that in several cases many of these subdivisions seemed to me entirely meaningless, but I thought that they were of some importance historically, and for a right appreciation of the methods of Indian philosophy. The long lists of the instruments and the acts of intellect, of the sources of activity, of Nescience with its sixty-two subdivisions, &c., though certainly meaningless to my mind, may possibly serve to show how long and how minutely these philosophical questions must have been discussed in order to leave such spoils behind. This large number of technical terms is certainly surprising. Some of them, as, for instance, SuM, Pada, Avadh&rita, &c., are not mentioned either in the Karikas or in the Sutras, and this, which has been taken for a sign of their more recent date, seems to me, on the contrary, to speak in favour of ,an early and independent origin of the Tattva- sam&sa and its commentary. If these technical terms were modern inventions, they would occur more frequently in modern works on the S&mkhya-philosophy, but as far as I know, they do not. * Mtlikfi-rtlias.

XVIII. We have still to examine, though as briefly as possible, the Mulik&rthas or eight cardinal facts, that is, the most important subjects established by the S&mkhya 2. They are with regard to Prakriti or Pradhana, its reality (Astitva), its oneness (Ekatva), its having an object or an 1 The names of the nine Tushfis or contentments are : Ambhas, water, Salila, Ogha, Vnshtf, Sutara, Supara, Sunetra, Sumari&ika, Uttam& Sattvikl. The names of the eight Siddhis are : Tara, Sutara, Tarayanti, Pramoda, Pramudita, Pramodamanay Kamyaka, Satpramudita.
a See Sawkhya-tattva-kaunmdi, p. 59.

SHASHn-TANTRA. ANTTGRAEA-SARGA. 2 7 1 intention (Arthavattva), and its being intended for some one else (Pararthya). They are with regard to Purusha his being different from Prakriti (Anyatva), his not being an agent (Akartritva), and his being many (Bahutva), They are with regard to both Prakriti and Purusha, their temporary union and separation, while Sthiti, durability, is said to refer to the Sukshma- and Sthula-sarlra, the gross and the subtle bodies. Astitva, reality, might seem to belong to both Praknti and Purusha, but it is meant as the reality of Prakriti only, which the Samkhya is chiefly concerned with establishing as against the Vedantins who deny it with regard to all that is objective, keepingjt for the subject only, whether he is called Purusha or Atman. The commentator, however, and Prof. Garbe also, connect Astitva with Purusha as well as with PrakritL The matter is of little consequence, unless Astitva Is taken in the sense of phenomenal or perceptible reality. The highest reality of the Purusha or the Atman has of course never been doubted by Samkhya or Vedanta philosophers, but that is more than mere Astitva.
Shashft-taixtra.

It should be added that the commentator in this place accounts once more for the name of Shashft-tantra, the Sixty-doctrine, but this time by adding the 17 Tushtfis and Siddhis, the 33 (Avidy& 5-f-Asakti 38) and 10, not 8, Mulikarthas, and thus arriving at 60 topics. The Chinese name presupposes a Saptati-sastra, or Seventy-treatise, probably with reference to the original number of verses in the Karika.
An-o.gr alia-saiga.

XIX. But even . here the Tattva-samasa is not yet finished, for it goes on to explain the Anugraha-sarga, lit. the creation of benevolence, which is explained as the production of external objects from the five Tanmatras or subtle essences for the sake of the Purusha* Brahma, after seeing these (the organs of sense?) produced, but as yet without a sphere in which their measuring or perceiving 272 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
power could find scope, created for them the so-called benevolent creation, shaped from the Tanm&tras1.
Bimta-sarga.

XX. After this follows the Bhuta-sarga in fourteen divisions. The divine creation has eight divisions, consisting of good and evil spirits and gods, such as Pis&&as, Rakshas, Yakshas, Gandharvas, Indra, Prac/apati, and Brahma. The animated creation consists of domestic animals, birds, wild animals, reptiles, and immovable things or plants. The human creation consists of one, of man only, from Brahmans down to ^TaTicZalas. Domestic animals are from cows down to mice; birds from Garucfe down to gnats; wild animals from lions down to jackals ; reptiles from $esha (world-serpent) down to worms ; immovable things from the Paiigata-tree (in paradise) down to grass. This is the threefold creation, consisting of gods, men, and animals, the animals, i. e. living beings, forming again five classes.
Bandlia, Bondage.

XXI. If it be asked what the threefold bondage (Bandha) consists in, it is replied, In the eight Prakratis, in the sixteen Vikaras, and in Dakshmi (gifts to priests). There are eight Prakntis, as often described before (pp. 244, 251) ; and as long as a man considers these as the highest, he is absorbed in Prakrit! and bound by Prakriti. The bondage of the sixteen Vikaras applies both to ascetics and to men of the world, if they are subdued by the senses, which are Vikaras, if they are devoted to objects of sense, if their organs of sense are not in subjection, if they are ignorant and deluded by passions.
Dakshina-'boxidag'e, Gifts to Priests.

The priestly bondage applies to those, whether householders, students, mendicants or anchorets, whose minds are overcome by passions and delusions, and who from misconception bestow sacrificial gifts on priests. A verse is quoted here in support : ' Bondage is spoken of by the 1 This passage is very doubtful, unless we connect Mana with Tanmatra, and take measuring in the sense of perceiving, so that the creation would be represented as made for man.

PBAKA^AS. 273 name of Prakriti-bondage, Vikara-bondage, and thirdly bondage through priestly gifts/ This last bondage seems to me very important, and it is strange that it should never have been pointed out as marking the unecclesiastical and unorthodox character of the Samkhya-philosophy J. What would have become of the Brahmans without their Daksmvftas or fees, the very name of a Brahman being DakshiTuya, one to be f ee'd ? In the Aitareya-Brahma?ia already we read of Yatis who condemned sacrifices, but they are said to have been thrown to the jackals. - That this feeing of a priest should have been considered one of the three bondages shows at all events that the followers of Kapila were above superstition, and looked upon sacrifice and priestcraft as hindrances rather than as helps to true freedom and Moksha of the spirit, Mokslta.

XXII. This Moksha, the highest aim of Kapila's philosophy, is again of three kinds, according as it arises from increase of knowledge, from the quieting of the passions of the senses, or lastly from the destruction of the whole. From increase of knowledge and quieting of the passions of the senses there arises the destruction of all that is commonly considered as merit and demerit ; and from the destruction of merit and demerit there arises final beatitude consisting in complete detachment from the world, and in concentration of the Purusha in himself.
Framanas.

XXIII. The three Pram^as which follow next require little explanation here, as they have been fully examined before2. Still each system of philosophy takes its own view of them, and the character of each is more or less determined by the view taken of the real nature of knowledge. What is most creditable is that each system should have recognised the importance of this question, as a preliminary to every philosophy. This distinguishes Indian philosophy very favourably from other philosophies. All systems of philosophy in India admit Pratyaksha or per- 1 See, however, Karlka 44. 2 p. 143- T 274 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

ceptlon of the senses as the first of Pramanas. The Vedanta, however, looks upon the Veda as the only source of true knowledge, and actually applies to it the name of Pratyaksha. The ordinary three or sis Prama^as of the MimamsS, would apply to the world of Avidya or nescience only, never to the true world of Brahman. See Vedanta- Sutras II, i, 14. The names vary sometimes, but the meaning is the same. Sensuous perception, if it is meant for what is perceived, is sometimes called Drz'sh£am, what is seen;^ and instead of Veda we meet with $abda, word, and Aptava&ana (Samkhya), right affirmation. Anumana, inference, is illustrated by the usual examples, such as, inference of rain from the rising of clouds, inference of water from the appearance of cranes, inference of fire from the rising of smoke. Whatever cannot bejproved by either sense or inference has to be accepted as Apta-va£ana, as, for instance, the existence of Indra, the king of the gods, the Northern Kurus, Mem, the golden mountain, the Apsaras, or nymphs of Svarga, &c. For all these things, Munis such as Vasish^a must be accepted as authorities. Apta is explained as a name for a man who is assiduous in his work, free from hatred and passion, learned, and endowed with all virtues, and who can therefore be relied upon. These three Pram&^as, or measures, are so called because in the same way as in common life grains are measured by measures such as a Prastha, and sandalwood, &c., weighed by a balance, the Tattvas also, the principles, the Bhavas (their modifications), and the Bhutas, elemental substances, are measured or proved by the XXIV. The last paragraph in the Tattva-samasa points back to the first. We saw in the beginning how a Br&hman was introduced who, overcome by threefold pain, took refuge with the great jffo'shi Kapila. If we ask what was meant by that threefold pain, the answer is that it is Adhyatrnika, Adhibhautika, and Adhidaivika. Adhyatmika is pain arising from the body, whether produced by wind, bile, or phlegm, &e., and from the mind (Manas), such as is due to desire, anger, greed, folly, envy, separation from THE TRUE MEANING OF THE SAJfKHYA. 275 what is liked, union with what is disliked, &c. Adhibhautika is pain that arises from other living beings, such as thieves, cattle, wild beasts, &c. Adhidaivika is pain that is caused by divine agents, as pain arising from cold, heat, wind, rain, thunderbolts, &c., all under the direction of the Vedic Devas. If a Brahman is affected by this threefold pain, a desire to know (the reason) arises in him, as a desire for water arises in a thirsty man. Freedom from pain, or final beatitude, is to be gained, as we are told, from a study of the Tattva-samasa. Whoever knows the philosophy which is contained in the Tattva-samasa, is not born again. This is the doctrine of the great sage Kapila, and thus is finished the commentary on the Sutras of the Tattva-samasa.
The True Meaning1 of the S&mkliya.

In giving an account of the Samkhya, I have followed entirely the Tattva-samasa, without mixing it up with the Karikas or Sutras. I was quite aware that the Karikas or the Sutras might have supplied us with a clearer and better-arranged account of that philosophy. But if I am right, that the Tattva-samasa is older than either, it seemed to me more important that we should know what the Samkhya really was in its original form. By comparing the Tattva-samasa with the Karikas and Sutras, we can easily see how this dry system was developed in later times. But though the Karikas and Sutras give us a more systematic account of the Samkhya, all that is essential can be found in the Samasa, if only we try to arrange the dry facts for ourselves. It must be confessed, no doubt, that neither in the Sutras, the K&rikas, nor in the Tattvasam&sa, do we find what we most value in every philosophy, an insight into the mind and heart of the founder of that philosophical system. If we were asked why such a system should ever have been imagined and elaborated, or what kind of comfort, whether intellectual or moral, it could have afforded to any human being, we should indeed have little to answer. All we can learn is that a man crushed by the burden of what is called the threefold misery, and seeing no hope of relief either by means of good actions or of sacrifices, which can promise no more than a temporary T 2 276 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY, happiness on earth or in Heaven, should seek advice from a philosopher, such as Kapila, believing that he could procure for him entire freedom from all his troubles.
zh: 阿毘婆（Avidyá）、無明（5）。

XIV. 什麼是五重無明（Avidya）？即無明、暗昧、迷妄、大迷妄、晦冥、極晦冥。此處暗昧與迷妄各有八重，大迷妄有十重，晦冥與極晦冥各有十八重。無明指暗昧，乃自我（Self）與非自我之物相同之誤解，即與實質（Prakriti）、未顯現者（Avyakta）、覺（Buddhi）、我執（Ahamkara）及五種細質（Tanmatras）相同之誤解。迷妄乃由獲得超自然力量（如微細性等）所引生之誤解。大迷妄乃一人自以為在音聲、色彩等十種對境狀態中已得解脫，不論其所聞所見等。晦冥乃對八種超人力量（如微細性等）及引生三種苦之十重感官世界所發之無節制仇恨。極晦冥乃在獲得八種人力並征服十重感官世界後，於死亡時刻所生起之痛苦。如是無明及其六十二重分支已得說明。
阿沙克蒂（Asakti），虛弱（28）。
XV. 何謂二十八重缺陷？乃十一感官之缺陷與智慧之十七缺陷。首先，就感官而言，耳有聾聵，舌有遲鈍，皮膚有癩疾，眼有盲目，鼻有嗅覺喪失，聲音有啞啞，手有跛足，足有跛足，排泄器官有便祕，生殖器官有陽痿，心有瘋狂；此乃十一感官之缺陷。智慧之十七缺陷乃滿足（Tushtfis, contentments）與完美（Siddhis, perfections）之對立面。

阿圖斯提與第十六。首先乃圖塞提（Tushii）或知足之對立面。彼等為： 阿南塔（Ananta），認為不存在原質（Pradhana, Prakriti）之確信；塔摩薩利那（Tamasalina），於大我（Mahat, Buddhi，理智）中認識阿特曼（Atman）之結合；阿毗笛雅（Avidyā），對自我（阿罕迦羅, Ahamkara）之非認識；阿毗利施提（Avrishti），否定細質（Tanmatras，本質）為元素之原因；阿蘇塔拉（Asutara），專心於獲取感官對象；阿蘇帕拉（Asupara），專心於其保存；阿蘇涅特拉（Asunetra），為獲取財富而專心，卻不見其易於喪失；阿蘇瑪里迦（Asumariika），沉溺於享樂；阿努塔瑪姆巴希卡（Anuttamambhasika），從事享樂而不見傷害（生命）之惡。如此，知足之九種對立面已得闡釋。
阿悉陀摩斯（Asiddhas）與悉地（Siddhis）。

XVII. 其次是悉地（Siddhi）即圓滿之相反物，亦稱為阿悉地（Asiddhis）即非圓滿：阿塔羅（Atara），乃將多樣性誤認為現象界統一之時；蘇塔羅（Sutara），乃僅聞言語後卻理解其相反之義，例如聞「知曉諸原理（tattvas）之人得解脫」之言後，卻理解為「此人未得解脫」；阿塔羅塔羅（Ataratara），乃無明，人雖精於聽聞與研習，卻因其遲鈍或智慧為邪說所損而未能認知二十五原理；阿普拉摩達（Apramoda），乃人雖為心苦所迫，卻不渴慕知識，對於輪迴漠然無知，使知識對其無有喜樂。由此應考慮阿普拉慕迪塔（Apramudita）即相互無喜樂與阿普拉摩達瑪那（Apramodamana）即相互不令喜樂之次一對。阿羅舍亞（Arasya），乃猶豫不決之人，即使對友人所教之事亦不知曉；阿薩特普拉慕迪塔姆（Asatpramuditam），乃不幸之人未得知識，或因教導不善，或因師長之忽視。如此八種阿悉地（Asiddhis）即圓滿之相反物已得說明，而二十八重阿薩克提（Asakti）即無力已告完成。
圖舒（Tush）及悉達（Siddhā）。

其次是圖什提（Tushii）與悉地（Siddhii）本身，但由於它們的對立面已經被檢視過，我們可以在此省略其列舉。這些技術術語在不同的文本中有所變化，但它們的重要性極其微小。我恐怕即使是我所提供的這些長列表——它們是數論哲學（Sāṃkhya-philosophy）極具特徵之處——也已經顯得極其冗長乏味，與哲學的偉大問題關係不大。我承認在許多情況下，這些細緻的分類對我而言似乎完全毫無意義，但我認為它們在歷史上具有重要意義，且對正確理解印度哲學的方法頗為必要。心識工具與作用的長列表、活動的來源、無明及其六十二個分類等，雖然對我的思想而言確實毫無意義，但可能有助於展示這些哲學問題必定經歷過多麼漫長與細緻的討論，才得以遺留下如此眾多的遺跡。這種大量的技術術語確實令人驚奇。其中有些術語，例如蘇美（Suṃ）、跋陀（Pada）、阿跋提列多（Avādhārita）等，既不見於《迦里迦》（Karikas）亦不見於《經文》（Sutras），而這一點被視為它們的較晚出現時期的標記，對我而言則相反，似乎傾向於支持《原則綱要》（Tattva-samāsa）及其註疏的早期與獨立的起源。若這些技術術語是現代的發明，它們應該更頻繁地出現在現代的數論哲學著作中，但據我所知，它們並未出現。*

XVIII. 我們仍須考查桑吉亞學派所確立的最重要主題，即穆利卡爾塔（Mulik&rthas）或八項根本事實，儘管盡可能簡潔地進行。這些事實關乎實質因（Prakriti）或原初質（Pradhana）的現實性（Astitva）、其一性（Ekatva）、其具有對象性或 --- **註：** 原文在此處未完成。根據手稿或原版，此段落應繼續討論其他七項根本事實的特性。頁下註 1 列舉了九種圖什提（Tusht&is）或知足的名稱：安跋斯（Ambhas，水）、薩利拉（Salila）、烏格哈（Ogha）、樂力什提（Vnshtf）、蘇塔拉（Sutara）、蘇帕拉（Supara）、蘇涅特拉（Sunetra）、蘇瑪麗卡（Sumarika）、烏塔瑪（Uttam&）、薩埵微妙（Sattvikl）。八種悉地（Siddhis）的名稱為：塔拉（Tara）、蘇塔拉（Sutara）、塔拉揚提（Tarayanti）、普拉莫達（Pramoda）、普拉姆迪塔（Pramudita）、普拉莫達曼亞（Pramodamanay）、卡姆亞卡（Kamyaka）、薩特普拉姆迪塔（Satpramudita）。
a 見《數論本性光論》（Sawkhya-tattva-kaunmdi），第59頁。

沙沙恩譚陀羅。安提格蕾亞薩爾迦。271 意圖（Arthavattva）及其為他人所欲求之性（Pararthya）。關於普魯沙（Purusha）者，為其異於實質（Anyatva）、其非行動者（Akartritva）及其為眾多性（Bahutva）。關於實質與普魯沙二者者，為其暫時之合與離。而住續性（Sthiti）據云乃指微細身與粗大身（Sukshma- 及 Sthula-sarīra）。有性（Astitva）似乎應屬於實質與普魯沙二者，然其旨在確立實質之有性，尤其針對於韋丹塔派，彼等否認客體之有性，而僅許可主體之有性，無論其稱為普魯沙或梵我（Atman）。然評注家及葛貝教授亦將有性與普魯沙以及實質相聯繫。此事若非將有性理解為現象的或可知覺的有性，則關係不大。普魯沙或梵我之最高有性誠然於數論派與韋丹塔派哲學家中從未有所懷疑，然而此乃超越於純粹的有性之上者。
我無法翻譯這段文字。 「Shashft-taixtra」似乎不是標準的英文、梵文或其他常見語言。這可能是： 1. 打字錯誤或轉錄誤差 2. 極罕見的梵文/古代語言術語 3. 無法辨識的片段 請提供正確的英文原文，我會按照您的規則進行專業翻譯。
應當補充說明，評註者在此處再次為《六十論》（Shashfi-tantra）的名稱提出解釋，但這次是加上17個圖什提（Tushtis）與悉地（Siddhis）、33個（無明與無力 Avidyā-s-Aśakti）及10個而非8個木利迦陀（Mulikarthas），藉此得出60個主題。中文名稱則預設了《七十論》（Saptati-śāstra），可能係參照《迦里迦》（Karika）原有的頌數而言。

XIX. 但即便在此，《本質摘要》（Tattva-samasa）仍未竟其功，蓋其進而闡釋安努格拉哈-薩爾加（Anugraha-sarga），字面義為「慈悲之創造」，被解釋為從五微質（五Tanmatras）或細微本質為普魯沙之故而產生外在對象，婆羅賀摩觀見此等（感知器官？）業已產生，然而猶缺乏一個領域使其衡量或知覺*得以進行。
力量得以發揮的空間，為他們創造了所謂的「仁慈創造」，乃由覺性微質（Tanmātras）塑造而成。
畢姆塔‧薩爾迦（Bimta-sarga）。

其後為不淨造化（Bhuta-sarga），分為十四部分。神聖造化分為八類，包括善惡精靈與諸神，如皮什查（Pisacas）、羅刹（Rakshas）、藥叉（Yakshas）、乾闥婆（Gandharvas）、因陀羅（Indra）、造物主（Prajapati）及梵天（Brahma）。生靈造化包括家畜、鳥類、野生動物、爬蟲類及不動之物或植物。人類造化僅由人組成，從婆羅門（Brahmans）至賤民（Pancalas）。家畜從牛至鼠；鳥類從迦樓羅（Garuda）至蚊蟲；野生動物從獅子至豺狼；爬蟲類從世蛇舍沙（Sesha，世界蛇）至蠕蟲；不動之物從天堂的五樹（Pañgata-tree）至草木。此為三重造化，由神、人、獸組成，其中獸即生靈，再分為五類。
班德利亞（Bandlia）、束縛（Bondage）。

XXI. 倘若有人問起三重束縛（Bandha）由何構成，答覆是：由八種自性（Prakratis）、十六種變化（Vikaras）以及祭祀供養（Dakshmi）而成。八種自性如前所多次述及（第244、251頁）；只要人視此等為最高者，他就沉溺於自性（Prakrit）之中，為自性所束縛。十六種變化的束縛既適用於苦行者，亦適用於在家人，倘若他們為感官所制服——此等感官乃變化之物——倘若他們執著於感官之對象，倘若他們的感覺器官未加制御，倘若他們為無明與情欲所迷惑。
達克希那（Dakshina）——「禮物」，獻予祭司的禮物。
祭司的束縛適用於那些心靈被激情與迷妄所征服的人，無論他們是居家者、學生、托缽僧或隱遁者，並且由於誤解而將祭祀供物贈予祭司。此處引述一句詩文以為佐證：「束縛乃由 --- ¹ 此段落殊為可疑，除非吾人將摩那（Mana）與譚摩多羅（Tanmatra）相連繫，並將「測量」理解為「感知」之義，如此創造便可被表述為為人類而作。

PBAKA^AS. 273 普拉克里蒂束縛、毗迦羅束縛之名，及其次祭司禮物之束縛。這最後一種束縛在我看來至為重要，而令人驚異的是，它竟未曾被指出為標誌著數論哲學（Samkhya-philosophy）之非教會性及異端性之特徵。若無其檀那那（Dakshvftas）或祭祀酬謝，婆羅門將何以為繼，婆羅門之名字本身即為檀那那（Daksina-ya），意即當被酬謝者？在《艾達勒耶婆羅門書》（Aitareya-Brahma?ia）中，吾人已讀及譴責祭祀之瑜伽行者（Yatis），然據言彼等已被投予豺狼。祭司被酬謝之舉竟被視為三種束縛之一，此事無論如何表明，迦毗羅之追隨者超越於迷信之上，視祭祀與祭司專斷為障礙而非助力，障礙而非助力於真正之自由及靈魂之解脫（Moksha）、解脫（Mokslta）。
XXII. 此莫克沙（Moksha）乃迦毘羅哲學之最高目標，復分為三種：其一源於知識之增長，其二源於感官情欲之寧靜，其三源於整體之毀滅。由知識之增長與感官情欲之寧靜，而生一切通常所謂之功德與罪業之毀滅；由功德與罪業之毀滅，而生最終之至福，包含對世界之完全超脫，以及普魯沙（Purusha）之自我專一。
婆羅門（Framanas）。
XXIII. 接下來的三個量證（Pramā）在此無需多加說明，蓋前已詳盡考察過矣。然而各個哲學體系對其各有獨特的見解，而每一體系的性格或多或少取決於對知識真實本質所持的見解。最值得稱許的是，各個體系都認識到這一問題的重要性，視其為一切哲學的先決條件。這一點使印度哲學明顯地優於其他哲學體系。印度的所有哲學體系都承認現量（Pratyaksha）或

感知為因明論（Pramanas）中的第一種。然而，吠檀多學派將吠陀視為真知識的唯一源頭，並實際上將「直接知覺」（Pratyaksha）這一名稱應用於吠陀。彌曼差學派普通的三種或六種因明論只適用於無明（Avidya）的世界，永遠不適用於梵（Brahman）的真實世界。見《吠檀多經》第二卷第一章第十四節。這些名稱有時各異，但含義相同。感官知覺，如果指的是所知覺的對象，有時被稱為「所見」（Drish'tam）；在吠陀的位置上，我們也會遇到「言語」（Sabda）和「正言」（Aptava'ana，數論派），這些術語。推論（Anumana）則以常見的例子來說明，例如從烏雲的升起推論降雨、從鶴鳥的出現推論有水、從煙的升起推論有火。凡是既不能由感知也不能由推論證明的事物，就必須被接受為「正言」（Apta-va'ana），例如因陀羅（諸神之王）的存在、北俱盧洲、妙翅鳥山、三十三天的仙女等。對於所有這些事物，必須接受瓦西什塔（Vasishtha）等聖人（Munis）為權威。「正言」（Apta）被解釋為指那些在工作中殷勤、無恨無欲、學識淵博、具備一切美德，因此可信賴的人。這三種因明論或「量」之所以被這樣稱呼，是因為就像在日常生活中穀物用「量器」（Prastha）來計量、檀香木等用天平來稱重一樣，原理（Tattvas）、變現（Bhavas）及元素物質（Bhutas）也是由「量」來計量或證明。 最後一段《原理要義》回溯到開篇。我們在開始時看到一位婆羅門被三重苦所困，遂投靠偉大的聖人迦毘羅（Kapila）。如果我們問那三重苦指的是什麼，答案是內苦、外苦和神苦。內苦是源於身體的苦楚，無論是由風、膽汁或痰液等引起，或是源於心識（Manas）的，如渴望、憤怒、貪婪、愚癡、嫉妒、與所樂分離、與所厭親近等所引發。外苦是源於其他生命體的苦楚，如盜賊、牲畜、野獸等所造成。神苦是由神聖力量造成的苦楚，如寒冷、炎熱、風、雨、雷電等所引發的痛苦，一切都在吠陀諸提婆的指導之下。若婆羅門為此三重苦所困，則對知識（其根由）的渴望就會在他心中生起，如渴水在渴者心中生起一樣。據說，從研習《原理要義》可以獲得免於痛苦的自由或最終的至福。凡知曉《原理要義》中所含之哲學者，不再轉生。此乃偉大聖人迦毘羅的教義，至此《原理要義經》之註疏乃告完成。

數論派的真正意義 --- 1 In the [*Rig-Veda*](梨俱吠陀), the word **Sâmkhya** (सांख्य) originally meant "calculation" or "enumeration." Later, in the philosophical schools of India, it came to denote a system of metaphysical thought that sought to explain the nature of reality through rational analysis and classification. The term itself derives from **Sankhya** (संख्या), meaning "number" or "count," reflecting its fundamental methodological commitment to systematic enumeration and logical categorization of the principles (*tattva*) underlying existence.

在描述數論派（Samkhya）時，我完全遵循《真理摘要》（Tattva-samasa），而未將其與《頌文》（Karikas）或《經文》（Sutras）混雜在一起。我深知《頌文》或《經文》本可為我們提供更清晰、更井然有序的該派哲學說明。然而，若我的判斷無誤，《真理摘要》確實早於兩者，那麼我們了解數論派在其原初形態中究竟為何便顯得更為重要。透過將《真理摘要》與《頌文》及《經文》進行比較，我們可以輕易觀察到這一枯燥的體系在後世如何得以發展。但儘管《經文》與《頌文》為我們提供了更具系統性的數論派說明，所有本質內容都可在《摘要》中找到，只要我們願意自行整理這些乾燥的事實。誠然必須承認的是，無論在《經文》、《頌文》或《真理摘要》中，我們都未能找到我們在每一種哲學中最重視的東西——對該哲學體系創立者心靈與心志的洞察。若有人問我們為何應當想像並發展出如此一個體系，亦或它能為任何人類提供何種慰藉，無論是智識上的抑或道德上的，我們確實將很難作答。我們所能習得的不過是：一個被所謂「三重苦難」所壓迫、看不到通過善行或祭祀獲得救助希望的人——因為祭祀最多只能承諾塵世或天界中短暫的幸福——應當求助於卡皮拉（Kapila）這樣的哲學家，相信他能為其帶來從所有煩惱中的完全解脫。
